_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 1
choices: It does not compile
It throws a NullPointerException
questions.explanation: `new` was called once, so there is exactly one object. `Student b = a;` copies the reference, not the object, so `a` and `b` are two names pointing at the same Student. Changing it through `b` changes what you see through `a`, and the answer is 2.0. If you picked 3.9, you read `b = a` as making a second student. It does not: only `new` builds an object, and there is no second `new` here.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 2
questions.prompt: The Student class declares `String name;` and `double gpa;` and has no constructor. What does this print?
choices: null then 0.0
It throws a NullPointerException on the first println
It does not compile, since the fields were never set
An empty line then 0
questions.explanation: `new` really did build an object, so `s` points at something and the dot works. Java fills unset fields with defaults: a `double` starts at 0.0, and any object type, including `String`, starts at `null`. So you get `null` and then `0.0`. If you picked the NullPointerException, you had the right idea about `null` but the wrong target: `s.name` is null, and `s` is not. Printing a null is fine. Only using a dot on a null crashes.

questions.title: Trace Classes & Objects
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: The whole file. `name` and `gpa` are fields: the state each Student carries, declared once in the class and then owned separately by every object built from it. `new Student()` builds one such object and hands back a reference to it, which is what `s` holds. The resulting output is 'Ada\n3.9'.

questions.title: Classes & Objects good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Classes & Objects?
choices: Name the class for the thing it represents, capitalized: `Student`, not `doStudentStuff`.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Name the class for the thing it represents, capitalized: `Student`, not `doStudentStuff`. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices: Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: Both sides compile, which is what makes this one sting: the mistake survives the compiler and only shows up when you run it. Declaring a variable of type Student does not create a Student, so the NullPointerException side holds a reference pointing at nothing. The dot in `s.name` says "go to the object and get its name", and there is no object to go to.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'You have been writing classes all along'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.

Every Java program you have written so far sat inside `public class Something { ... }`. That was not decoration. Java has nowhere to put code except inside a class, so even a one line program needs one. Today the class stops being a wrapper you ignore and becomes the thing you are actually designing.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Every Java program you have written so far sat inside `public class Something { ... }`. That was not decoration. Java has nowhere to put code except inside a class, so even a one line program needs one. Today the class stops being a wrapper you ignore and becomes the thing you are actually designing.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 7
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'What `this` is for'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.

In that constructor, the parameter is called `name` and the field is also called `name`. Java resolves the plain name `name` to the parameter, because the closer one wins. So `this.name = name` means: take the value that was passed in, and store it in this object's field. Drop the `this.` and you write `name = name`, which quietly assigns the parameter to itself and leaves the field empty. It compiles. It just does nothing.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: In that constructor, the parameter is called `name` and the field is also called `name`. Java resolves the plain name `name` to the parameter, because the closer one wins. So `this.name = name` means: take the value that was passed in, and store it in this object's field. Drop the `this.` and you write `name = name`, which quietly assigns the parameter to itself and leaves the field empty. It compiles. It just does nothing.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 8
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'A NullPointerException means you never called new'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.

This is the error Java beginners hit most. It does not mean the object is broken. It means there is no object. Some reference is holding `null`, and you used a dot on it. Java tells you which line, so go to that line, find the variable before the dot, and trace backwards to where it should have been given a `new`. Usually you declared it in one branch of an `if` and used it outside.
questions.explanation: This is the error Java beginners hit most. It does not mean the object is broken. It means there is no object. Some reference is holding `null`, and you used a dot on it. Java tells you which line, so go to that line, find the variable before the dot, and trace backwards to where it should have been given a `new`. Usually you declared it in one branch of an `if` and used it outside.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Classes & Objects?
choices: Fields are what it knows. Methods are what it can do. Sort your ideas into those two piles first.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Fields are what it knows. Methods are what it can do. Sort your ideas into those two piles first. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 10
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Classes & Objects?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Write a constructor as soon as an object needs more than one field, so an object can never exist half filled in.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Write a constructor as soon as an object needs more than one field, so an object can never exist half filled in. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.
